skill.name: circos-plot-generator
skill.description: Generate Circos configuration files for circular genomics data visualization. Supports genomic variations (SNPs, CNVs, structural variants), cell-cell communication networks, and custom track configurations for publication-ready circular plots.
skill.summary: Automate Circos configuration generation for circular genomics data visualization and publication-ready plots.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 152 static analysis findings are false positives. The subprocess.run call at scripts/main.py:498 invokes the legitimate Circos bioinformatics tool with hardcoded arguments (no command injection vector). Markdown backtick formatting in SKILL.md was incorrectly classified as Ruby shell execution (103 false positives). Chromosome size keys and argparse help text strings were misidentified as cryptographic material. URLs in SKILL.md point to the skill's own GitHub repository and official Circos documentation. No environment variable access exists - the flagged locations read from a Python configuration dictionary parameter. No prompt injection or data exfiltration patterns were detected. The skill is a legitimate bioinformatics tool for generating Circos visualization configurations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in SKILL.md are legitimate references
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged 4 URLs in SKILL.md as network risk. These are legitimate external resource links: GitHub source repository (line 7), Circos official documentation (line 976), Circos tutorials (line 977), Bioconda Circos recipe (line 978), and Nature Genetics examples (line 979). No data exfiltration or unauthorized network activity. URLs are passive references only.

low_findings.title: subprocess.run calls Circos with hardcoded arguments
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged subprocess.run at scripts/main.py:498 as external command execution. This calls the Circos bioinformatics rendering tool with hardcoded arguments ['circos', '-conf', config_path]. No user input is injected into the command. The cwd is set to the user-specified output directory. Error handling via try/except prevents crashes. This is the core purpose of the skill - rendering generated Circos configurations.

low_findings.title: Markdown code formatting misidentified as shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged 103 locations in SKILL.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. SKILL.md is a Markdown documentation file where backticks are used for code formatting (inline `code` and ```fenced blocks```). No actual shell execution occurs. Similarly, backticks in references/runtime_checklist.md lines 3 and 8 are Markdown formatting.

low_findings.title: Config dictionary access misidentified as environment access
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged 9 locations in scripts/main.py as 'env_access'. All flagged locations read from self.config.get(...) where config is a Python dictionary parameter passed to CircosConfig.__init__. This is standard object configuration access, not environment variable access. Examples: config.get('output', '.'), config.get('data'), config.get('tracks', []).

low_findings.title: Argparse help text misidentified as weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged scripts/main.py lines 430-431 and multiple SKILL.md lines as 'Weak cryptographic algorithm'. Line 430 is parser.add_argument description='Generate Circos...' and line 431 is formatter_class=argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter. These are standard Python argparse strings, not encryption. SKILL.md lines flagged are markdown table separators and YAML front matter text.

low_findings.title: Chromosome keys misidentified as certificate files
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged scripts/main.py:102 and :454 as 'Certificate/key files'. Line 102 accesses CHROMOSOME_SIZES.keys() which returns chromosome names (chr1, chr2, ..., chrX, chrY) - standard human genome reference data. Line 454 accesses COLOR_SCHEMES.keys() which returns color scheme names (default, nature, lancet, cell). Neither relates to security certificates.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run for Circos rendering
dangerous_patterns.description: scripts/main.py:498 calls subprocess.run(['circos', '-conf', config_path], capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=args.output). Arguments are hardcoded and the call is safely wrapped in try/except for FileNotFoundError. The Circos binary is a standard bioinformatics tool. No command injection vector exists.

content.user_title: Generate Circular Genomics Plots with Circos
content.value_statement: Creating Circos visualization configurations for genomics data requires expertise in Perl and complex configuration syntax. This skill automates configuration generation for genomic variations, cell-cell communication networks, and custom tracks, producing publication-ready circular plots without manual Circos coding.
actual_capabilities: Generate Circos configuration files for visualizing SNPs, CNVs, and structural variants on chromosome ideograms
Create cell-cell communication network plots with ribbon tracks connecting interacting cell types
Support multiple Circos track types including histograms, scatter plots, links, heatmaps, and text annotations
Generate sample data files for quick testing and validation of configuration outputs
Render completed configurations to PNG or SVG using the Circos visualization engine
Apply predefined color schemes (Nature, Lancet, Cell) for publication-ready styling
limitations: Requires Circos and Perl to be installed separately for rendering configuration files to images
Limited to configuration generation for supported plot types (variation, cell-communication, custom)
Does not perform genomic data analysis or variant calling from raw sequencing data
Large datasets with many tracks may require manual optimization of configuration parameters

use_cases.title: Publication-Ready Genomic Variation Figures
use_cases.description: Generate Circos configurations for visualizing SNPs, CNVs, and structural variants across chromosomes. Apply publication color schemes and output configurations ready for final rendering.
use_cases.target_user: Research scientists creating figures for genomics manuscripts

use_cases.title: Cell-Cell Communication Network Visualization
use_cases.description: Create circular network plots showing intercellular interactions and signaling pathways from single-cell data. Visualize connection strength and directionality between cell types.

use_cases.title: Custom Track Annotation for Circos Plots
use_cases.description: Configure multi-track circular plots with histograms, heatmaps, scatter points, and text labels for complex genomics data presentation. Combine multiple data types in a single visualization.
use_cases.target_user: Computational biologists creating multi-dimensional genomic visualizations

prompt_templates.title: Basic Genomic Variation Plot
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Circos configuration for genomic variation data. Use the variation plot type with chromosome ideograms showing SNP density and CNV regions. Apply the Nature color scheme and set the output resolution to 1200x1200 pixels.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a standard genomic landscape plot from variant call data

prompt_templates.title: Cell-Cell Communication Network
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cell-cell communication Circos plot from interaction data. Use the cell-comm plot type to visualize signaling pathways between cell types. Show connection strength with ribbon width and color intensity.
prompt_templates.scenario: Visualizing ligand-receptor interactions between cell populations

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Track Custom Annotation
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a custom Circos plot with three tracks: a histogram track for gene expression values, a scatter track for p-values, and a link track for chromatin interactions. Use the Lancet color scheme.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a complex multi-track figure for a genomics study

prompt_templates.title: Batch Configuration Generation with Sample Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate sample variation data and create a Circos configuration in one step. Create the sample data first, then build the configuration with the Nature color scheme at 1500x1500 resolution. Explain how to render the output.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick prototyping and testing of Circos plot layout before using real data

output_examples.input: Generate a genomic variation Circos plot showing SNPs on chr1 through chr22 with the Nature color scheme
output: Karyotype file with 24 chromosome definitions written
Variation tracks generated: 1 histogram track for SNP density, 1 link track for structural variants
Data files created in ./circos_output/data/ with variations and links in Circos-compatible format
Command to render: cd ./circos_output && circos -conf circos.conf

output_examples.input: Create a cell-cell communication network plot from sample immune cell interaction data
output: Circos configuration generated for cell-cell communication visualization
Custom karyotype created with 6 immune cell types as chromosome bands
Ribbon tracks generated showing interaction strength between cell type pairs
Label tracks added for each cell type with positioning at 50r from center
Output configured for both PNG and SVG formats at 800x800 resolution

best_practices: Generate SVG output for publication figures - SVGs are scalable and editable in vector graphics software
Archive both the configuration files and input data alongside the rendered images to ensure reproducibility
Validate your data format matches the expected TSV columns (chrom, start, end, type, value) before generating plots
anti_patterns: Using overly large data files without filtering - Circos performance degrades with very dense tracks; aggregate or bin your data first
Setting all tracks to the same radial position - overlapping tracks produce unreadable plots; use distinct r0/r1 ranges for each track
Rendering without running a quick validation test first - always generate a sample plot to verify configuration before committing to full resolution output

faq.question: Do I need Circos installed to use this skill?
faq.answer: No, the skill generates configuration files that do not require Circos to create. You only need Circos installed if you want to render the configurations into PNG or SVG images.

faq.question: What data formats are supported for input files?
faq.answer: The skill accepts TSV and CSV files. For variation plots, columns include chrom, start, end, type, and value. For cell-communication plots, columns include source, target, and weight.

faq.question: What types of genomic variations can be visualized?
faq.answer: The skill supports SNPs, CNVs, structural variants including translocations and inversions, deletions and duplications. Each variation type has a distinct color and visualization style.

faq.question: Can I customize the colors of the plot?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill provides four built-in color schemes: default, Nature, Lancet, and Cell. You can select a scheme when generating the configuration.

faq.question: What is the difference between generating and rendering?
faq.answer: Generating creates the Circos configuration text files. Rendering uses the Circos software to convert those configuration files into PNG or SVG images. The skill always generates configurations and optionally renders if Circos is installed.

faq.question: Does this skill work with data from other bioinformatics tools?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill integrates with upstream tools like CNV callers and CRISPR screen analyzers. It produces standard Circos configuration files that can be used with any Circos-compatible pipeline.
